domains.title: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration
points: Coordinator/subagent pattern: a coordinator delegates to specialized subagents and explicitly passes outputs forward — subagents don't share memory or context automatically.
Parallelize independent subagent calls (e.g. web search + document analysis with no shared dependency) instead of invoking them sequentially, to cut latency.
Reflection / iterative loops: let the coordinator evaluate an agent's output for gaps and re-delegate with a targeted follow-up before moving on, rather than proceeding linearly.
Keep orchestration centralized in the coordinator — avoid letting subagents spawn their own subagents or share tools outside their specialization; it hurts observability and control.
For crash recovery, persist each agent's structured output to a known location so the coordinator can reload a manifest and re-inject only relevant state, instead of replaying full conversation logs.
Route simple queries around the full pipeline (query complexity routing) instead of always traversing every subagent.
Escalation and max-turn handling: define an explicit exit condition (resolve or hand off to a human) so an agentic loop can't exhaust its turn budget mid-task without a resolution.

domains.title: Tool Design & MCP Integration
points: Distinguish transient errors (timeouts, 503s — safe to retry) from permanent/business errors (validation failures, permission denied — retrying never helps); return distinct error types so the agent's retry logic can tell them apart.
Write instructive tool error messages: state what went wrong and what to try next, not just "failed".
Use dry-run / preview parameters for destructive or high-stakes tools, and enforce that the agent actually surfaces the preview to the user before executing for real.
As a tool library grows, tool selection accuracy drops — mitigate with a search_connectors-style discovery tool, or by narrowing which tools are exposed per subagent/context.
Prefer structured JSON output over free-text tool results when the caller needs to reliably parse specific fields downstream.
MCP server scope: project-level config for servers the whole team should share, user-level config for personal/experimental servers.
Tool descriptions should disambiguate similarly-named or similarly-behaved tools (e.g. archive vs delete) so the agent doesn't pick the wrong one under ambiguous phrasing.

domains.title: Claude Code Configuration & Workflows
points: CLAUDE.md conventions aren't always followed reliably from prose alone — conflicting or overly long CLAUDE.md files (500+ lines) degrade adherence; split into scoped nested CLAUDE.md files per subdirectory instead.
Slash commands (e.g. /migrate-component) are the way to share a reusable, versioned workflow across a team — store them in the project so they stay in sync as the team iterates.
Use grep/content-search tools for locating patterns across a large codebase (e.g. finding every eval() call) rather than reading files individually.
For big, unfamiliar changes, build understanding first (trace call paths, read related modules) before editing — especially across 15+ files or 8,000+ lines.
Session/context resumption: when picking up multi-day work, re-establish the relevant investigation context explicitly rather than assuming Claude Code remembers prior sessions.
Iterative refinement workflows suit ambiguous, open-ended, or performance-sensitive tasks (e.g. "build a caching layer") better than one-shot generation.

Automated code review prompts: broad single-prompt reviewers trade off recall vs. precision — split by concern (security, API design, business logic) or tune independently per concern to fix an under-performing category without hurting the others.

domains.title: Prompt Engineering & Structured Output
points: Strict JSON schemas eliminate syntax errors but not semantic errors — validation-retry loops must check semantic correctness (e.g. totals reconcile, enums cover real-world values), not just "does it parse".

Retry-with-error-feedback resolves most transient/format failures within a few attempts, but is ineffective against systematic gaps — e.g. a field the model has no way to know (unstated in the source), which needs a schema or workflow change, not more retries.
Enum fields should include an "other" / catch-all option when the real-world value space is wider than the sampled schema, to avoid validation failures on legitimate-but-unlisted values.
Nullable fields left unspecified in a source document should stay null — watch for the model fabricating plausible-but-incorrect values instead of returning null when information is genuinely absent.
Large tool/schema definitions competing with a large document for context budget degrades extraction accuracy as documents approach the context limit, even well under the max.

Batch APIs (delayed processing, discounted cost) are the right call when your SLA allows an hours-long window — but plan for the reprocessing path when a subset fails (e.g. context_length_exceeded on batch items).
Human review should be triaged by confidence score, but confirm that high-confidence errors are actually rare with data before fully automating away review at any given threshold.

domains.title: Context Management & Reliability
points: Long conversations degrade recall of specific details (names, numbers, earlier preferences) well before the context window is technically full — sliding-window truncation alone loses information rather than compressing it.

Persistent facts (identity, standing preferences, safety-critical details like allergies) need to be extracted and carried forward explicitly, separate from ephemeral back-and-forth that can be safely dropped.
System prompt adherence drifts over long conversations (10-30+ turns) — re-anchoring persona/instructions periodically, or summarizing+re-injecting core directives, counters this drift.
Tool call outputs (e.g. large JSON blobs from a lookup tool) can dominate context faster than the conversation itself — trim or summarize stale tool results once they're no longer needed.

When a live external event happens mid-conversation (e.g. a webhook), the assistant needs that update injected into context so its next response reflects current reality without the user having to state it.
Multi-session continuity (memory across days/weeks) requires deliberate persistence of key facts between sessions — new conversations don't automatically inherit prior session context.

Balance clarifying questions against action: too many questions before acting causes abandonment; too few causes acting on wrong assumptions — tune to the cost of guessing wrong for that specific task.
